title: How to Use Unique Fields in GoHighLevel — Prevent Data Duplication
description: Learn how to use Unique Fields in GoHighLevel Custom Objects to prevent duplicate records and maintain data integrity. Step-by-step setup guide inside.
html_content:
Data duplication is one of the silent killers of CRM efficiency. You think you're tracking leads properly, but then you discover three records for the same contact—each with conflicting information, different interaction histories, and inconsistent statuses. It's a nightmare to clean up, and it tanks your reporting accuracy.

The Unique Fields feature in GoHighLevel's Custom Objects exists to prevent exactly this problem. By marking specific fields as unique, you ensure that certain values can only exist once across your entire custom object. This means no duplicate phone numbers, no repeated email addresses, no accidental re-entry of the same reference ID.

In this guide, I'll walk you through how to set up and manage unique fields, why they matter for data integrity, and the real-world scenarios where they save you hours of cleanup work. If you're running an agency or managing client data at scale, this feature alone can transform how clean your database stays.

Ready to experience the power of a unified CRM platform? Get a free 30-day trial of GoHighLevel — that's double the standard trial — and start building a bulletproof data management system today.

📋 In This Guide

  • What Are Unique Fields in GoHighLevel?
  • How to Mark Fields as Unique in Custom Objects
  • Which Field Types Support Unique Constraints
  • Rules, Limits, and Best Practices
  • Real-World Scenarios Where Unique Fields Prevent Disaster
  • Troubleshooting Duplicate Record Issues

What Are Unique Fields in GoHighLevel?

A unique field is a data constraint that forces a specific field value to appear only once within a custom object. Once a value is entered in that field, no other record in the same custom object can contain that exact same value.

Think of it like a fingerprint system for your data. Just as no two people share the same fingerprint, no two records can share the same unique field value. This is particularly powerful for identifiers—things like:

  • Reference IDs or tracking numbers (e.g., "REF-12345")
  • Email addresses (within a specific custom object)
  • Phone numbers (for vendor records, client numbers, etc.)
  • License plate numbers, serial numbers, or SKUs
  • Customer account numbers assigned by external systems

When you set a field as unique, GoHighLevel will actively prevent you from creating or updating a record with a duplicate value. The system will throw an error and block the entry—which stops bad data from entering your database in the first place.

💡 Pro Tip

Unique fields are different from standard validation. Validation checks format (like "must be an email"). Unique fields check existence (like "this email can only exist once"). Use both together for maximum data protection.

How to Mark Fields as Unique in Custom Objects

Setting up a unique field in GoHighLevel is straightforward. Here's the exact process:

Step 1: Navigate to Custom Objects

Go to your GoHighLevel workspace and find the Custom Objects section in your dashboard. Select the specific custom object where you want to enforce uniqueness.

Step 2: Open the Field Editor

Click the field you want to mark as unique. If it's a new field, create it first by adding a new field to the custom object. Then click the settings icon or edit option for that field.

Step 3: Enable the Unique Constraint

Look for the "Make Unique" or "Unique Field" toggle. In most versions, this appears as a checkbox in the field settings. Enable it.

Step 4: Save Changes

Click save. GoHighLevel will now enforce uniqueness on that field for all new records and updates going forward.

That's it. From that moment on, the system prevents duplicate entries in that field across your entire custom object.

Which Field Types Support Unique Constraints

Not every field type in GoHighLevel supports unique constraints. Here's what does:

Fully Supported Field Types:

  • Text (single-line text fields)
  • Email
  • Phone
  • Number (integers and decimals)
  • URL

Conditionally Supported:

  • Dropdown/Select (in most cases, though results vary by implementation)

Not Supported:

  • Long text / Rich text
  • Date fields
  • Checkbox
  • Multi-select
  • Relationship fields
  • File uploads

The reason is technical: GoHighLevel's database indexes work best with discrete, finite values. Text and numbers are ideal. Dates and large text blocks create indexing conflicts that would slow performance.

Rules, Limits, and Best Practices

The Core Rules of Unique Fields

Once you mark a field as unique, these rules take effect:

  • Null values are allowed: If a field is optional and left blank, multiple records can have blank values. The uniqueness constraint only applies to non-empty entries.
  • Case sensitivity varies: Text fields are generally case-insensitive for uniqueness ("john@example.com" and "John@example.com" are treated as duplicates).
  • No retroactive enforcement: If you mark a field as unique after records already exist with duplicates, the system won't automatically merge or delete those records. You'll need to clean them up manually first.
  • System-wide scope: Uniqueness applies across the entire custom object, not just within a specific user, account, or location.

Best Practices for Implementation

1. Clean Your Data First
Before enabling uniqueness on an existing field, audit your data for duplicates. Export records, identify conflicts, and resolve them. Trying to enforce uniqueness on messy data is like putting a lock on a door that's already been broken into.

2. Use Unique Fields for Identifiers Only
Reserve unique constraints for fields that truly need to be one-of-a-kind: reference numbers, external IDs, serial numbers. Don't mark common fields like "Company Name" as unique unless you're certain no two records should share that value.

3. Make Required Fields + Unique Fields Together
If a field is unique, consider making it required. This prevents users from accidentally creating "blank" records that later can't be matched to data imports.

4. Plan for API and Bulk Imports
If you use GoHighLevel's API or bulk import features, ensure your integration respects unique field constraints. Failed imports due to duplicate detection are frustrating—catch these issues before they reach production.

Real-World Scenarios Where Unique Fields Prevent Disaster

Scenario 1: Lead Duplication from Multiple Sources

You're running ads, landing pages, and form submissions across multiple platforms. A prospect fills out your form twice, or their data syncs from your ad platform AND your CRM integration. Without a unique field, you get two records. With a unique "Email" field, the system rejects the second entry (or prompts you to update the existing one instead).

Scenario 2: Vendor Management

You're managing vendor records. Each vendor has a unique "Vendor ID" assigned by your accounting system. Without uniqueness, an admin might accidentally create two vendor records with the same ID, causing confusion in reporting and payments. With a unique field, it's impossible.

Scenario 3: Subscription/Membership Tracking

You're tracking customer subscriptions with a unique "Subscription ID." When a customer renews, you need to update the existing subscription record, not create a new one. A unique field ensures the system knows which record to update when that subscription ID re-enters your system.

Scenario 4: Multi-Agency Client Management

As an agency, you manage clients across multiple team members. Each client has a unique "Client Reference Number." If your team doesn't coordinate, someone might add the same client twice. With a unique field, the second entry is blocked, forcing the team to discover the existing record.

Troubleshooting Duplicate Record Issues

What If GoHighLevel Says a Value Already Exists, but You Don't See It?

Check these things first:

  • Check for whitespace: Look for hidden spaces before or after values. "john@example.com " (with a trailing space) is technically different from "john@example.com" but looks the same.
  • Check deleted records: In some systems, soft-deleted records still count as existing. Verify whether GoHighLevel has truly removed the record or just marked it as deleted.
  • Case sensitivity: Confirm your text field comparison is case-insensitive where needed.
  • Reload your view: Sometimes the UI doesn't refresh immediately. Refresh your browser or navigate away and back to the custom object.

What If I Need to Disable a Unique Constraint?

If you realize a field doesn't need to be unique anymore, you can disable the constraint in the field settings. GoHighLevel won't delete your existing duplicate values—it simply stops preventing new ones. You'll need to clean up any existing duplicates manually if you later want to enforce uniqueness again.

Frequently Asked Questions

Can I mark multiple fields as unique in the same custom object?

Yes. You can mark as many fields as unique as you need. Each field enforces its own uniqueness constraint independently. For example, you could have both "Email" and "Customer ID" marked as unique in the same custom object.

What happens if I try to import data that violates a unique field constraint?

GoHighLevel will reject those records during the import process. You'll receive an error report showing which records failed due to uniqueness violations. You'll then need to either remove the duplicates from your import file or update the existing records instead of creating new ones.

Does a unique field constraint apply across different custom objects?

No. Uniqueness is enforced only within a single custom object. If you have "Vendor ID" marked as unique in your "Vendors" custom object, you can still have the same "Vendor ID" in your "Contracts" custom object if needed.

Can I undo marking a field as unique if I already have duplicate values?

Yes, you can disable the unique constraint at any time. However, if you want to re-enable it later and you have duplicates in that field, you'll need to manually clean up the duplicates first before the system will allow re-enforcement.

Are unique fields case-sensitive?

Generally, no—GoHighLevel treats text-based unique fields as case-insensitive. "John" and "john" are treated as the same value. However, always test this in your specific setup to confirm.
